questions.question: What are the three components of self-compassion?

options.text: Self-esteem, confidence, and optimism
Self-kindness, common humanity, and mindfulness
Acceptance, forgiveness, and gratitude
Relaxation, positive thinking, and social support

questions.explanation: Psychologist Kristin Neff identified three core components of self-compassion: self-kindness (treating yourself with warmth rather than harsh criticism), common humanity (recognizing that suffering and imperfection are universal human experiences), and mindfulness (holding painful experiences with balanced awareness without over-identifying with them).

questions.question: What does research show about the relationship between self-compassion and motivation?

options.text: Self-compassion significantly reduces motivation and drive
Self-compassion has no effect on motivation
Self-compassion maintains or increases motivation while reducing the distress associated with self-criticism
Self-compassion only helps with motivation in creative fields

questions.explanation: Contrary to perfectionists' biggest fear, research consistently shows that self-compassion does not reduce motivation. Instead, it maintains or increases motivation — especially willingness to try after failure and persistence through difficulty — while reducing anxiety, depression, and shame. Self-compassion creates motivation based on caring rather than fear.

questions.question: How is self-compassion different from self-pity?

options.text: There is no real difference
Self-pity isolates ('Poor me, I have it worse') while self-compassion connects to shared humanity ('This is hard, and everyone struggles sometimes')
Self-compassion is only for minor problems while self-pity is for serious ones
Self-pity is healthy while self-compassion is not

questions.explanation: Self-pity says 'Poor me, I have it worse than everyone else' — it isolates you and emphasizes how uniquely you are suffering. Self-compassion says 'This is hard, and it is hard for many people. I deserve kindness in this moment' — it connects you to the shared human experience of struggle and imperfection.

questions.question: What is the 'self-compassion break' used for?

options.text: Taking a break from work when feeling stressed
A brief in-the-moment practice using three phrases — one for mindfulness, common humanity, and self-kindness — when you notice self-criticism
A weekly scheduled therapy session
A technique for avoiding difficult emotions

questions.explanation: The self-compassion break is a brief in-the-moment practice for when you notice self-criticism arising. It uses three phrases representing the three components: 'This is a moment of suffering' (mindfulness), 'Suffering is part of being human' (common humanity), and 'May I be kind to myself' (self-kindness). It can be combined with a physical gesture like placing a hand on your heart.

questions.question: How do self-compassion and cognitive restructuring work together?

options.text: They are competing approaches and you should only use one
Cognitive restructuring changes the content of your thoughts while self-compassion changes the tone of your inner voice — together they are more effective
Self-compassion replaces cognitive restructuring entirely
They work the same way and produce identical results

questions.explanation: Cognitive restructuring and self-compassion complement each other: cognitive restructuring addresses the content of your thoughts (what you believe — e.g., challenging 'I am a failure'), while self-compassion addresses the tone (how you talk to yourself — e.g., replacing harsh criticism with kindness). Using both together creates more lasting change than either alone.

title: Self-Compassion as an Antidote to Perfectionism - Quiz
